claims.claim_text: Opening healthcare-startup regression anecdote about a four-person ML team, a copied internal API model, and a customer-reported regression three months later.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph
claims.anchor_text: A four-person ML team at a healthcare startup had a model that looked excellent in a notebook, passed a local evaluation script, and was quietly copied into an internal API by the only engineer who knew how the artifact was produced. Three months later, a customer-reported regression forced the team to ask basic questions under pressure: which run produced the model, which dataset snapshot was used, which prompt template changed, who approved the deployment, and whether the previous artifact could be restored.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A small ML team can end up with a model that looks good in a notebook, gets copied into an internal API, and later becomes difficult to explain or restore because the run, data snapshot, prompt template, and deployment history were never recorded.
claims.rationale: This is a specific operational anecdote with precise team size, sector, and timeline but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: MLflow began as an open-source project for the full ML lifecycle, and small teams often adopt only tracking and artifact features first.
claims.anchor_text: **MLflow began as an open-source project for the full ML lifecycle**: Small teams often adopt only its tracking and artifact features first, which is a reasonable starting point when the immediate problem is traceability rather than complete platform automation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **MLflow is an open-source platform with lifecycle capabilities for ML and AI systems**: For a small team focused on traceability, starting with tracking and artifact features can be a reasonable first step before adding more platform automation.
claims.rationale: The MLflow repository supports the platform/lifecycle description, but the claim about what small teams often adopt first is a teaching generalization rather than a directly sourced fact.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Jobs are often enough for early shared training, and one Job can standardize image, command, resources, logs, and failure behavior.
claims.anchor_text: **Kubernetes Jobs are often enough for early shared training**: A team does not need a full workflow engine to stop relying on personal laptops; one well-defined Job can standardize image, command, resources, logs, and failure behavior.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Kubernetes Jobs can be an early way to standardize shared training**: A well-defined Job can give a team one repeatable way to run shared training workloads outside personal laptops without adopting a full workflow engine first.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes Job docs support the run-to-completion workload primitive, but they do not directly support the broader sufficiency claim that Jobs are 'often enough' for early platform design.

further_reading.title: MLflow Repository
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful primary reference for MLflow's tracking, artifact, and lifecycle capabilities in small-team MLOps setups.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Jobs
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines the native Kubernetes primitive the module recommends for repeatable training and evaluation execution.

further_reading.title: Using RBAC Authorization
further_reading.why_relevant: Supports the module's access-control and ownership discussion for shared platform components.

notes: Most of the module is pedagogical guidance, diagrams, code samples, and exercises; the main inline-citation needs are the unsourced opening anecdote and the two 'Did You Know' bullets that make concrete product/capability claims.
